Maybe you're helping them to lose weight or you're helping them to do something different. Like I have a friend who, his coaching program helps people get trafficking to their funnels. Okay, and he knows his, I think he sells his program for $15,000. So he knows that if there aren't any money, I don't want to call them on the phone. So he asked me, I said, how much do you currently spend each month in advertising? Zero to 5,000, five to 10, 10 to whatever, 50, 50 to 100. So he says, you know, if someone's always spending zero to five grand in ads, it means they're probably not going to be able to afford $15,000. So I'm gonna teach them how to use ads, right? So he's using that question to qualify. So this person can have the money for me to actually get on the phone. Cause you call every single person. A lot of times, you know, your sales person, people get burned out, things like that. So I try to find a question to help qualify how much revenue they're actually making to see if they're going to be a good fit for my program. Okay, so that's the first thing is, is a revenue qualifier question. And it's not directly like, how much money do you make? Typically, I have seen some people do that, which is totally cool. But I usually try to do it kind of a more roundabout way and just have themselves select where they fit. So I kind of understand about where their financial situation is before we even pick up the call. Number two, is I want to understand for them, if they come into this group, what does success look like for them at the end? Okay, because if you come in and people like, my goal, you know, they get my program, my success for me means I made $100 million. I'll be like, you're not good fit for this program. Like we may be able to do that, but like I have no idea. And if we don't hit 100 million and you're gonna be upset, like I don't want you in the program. Like I want to find out what does success actually look like for you the next 12 months? Like what's the realistic look of that? My favorite question, I will give you specifics on this question, because it's one of my favorites. If you read the book, The Question by Dan Sullivan, this is the question he posed that he uses for all of his coaching clients. And I put in every one of my applications because I think it's the best way for them to really get a good view of what success is gonna look like. So this is the question. He says, if we were having this discussion three years from today and you were looking back over the last three years working with me, what would have happened in your life, both personally and professionally, for you to feel happy with your progress? So I always ask people that question. And for me, you use that short of from three years down to a year, because I want to know in a year what's it gotta look like that's gonna be successful. You know, if I ask them what's your goals, everyone's got this big, huge goal. I want to make a billion dollars, I want to lose a thousand pounds or whatever it is. If I say look in a year from now, you get this program for a year, and we look back, what'll have to have happened if you be successful? And then people get more honest. I'm like, well, you know, if I was able to pay off my debt, that'd be awesome. If I had more time with my wife, that'd be great. And they kind of give more realistic things in a year. And so that's the question. If you want to go deeper and to read the book, the question by Dan Sullivan, it's one of my favorites, and he just goes, like, the psychology behind this question, how many doors it opens up, but it helps you understand exactly what they're looking for to be a client, and then you know exactly how to serve them as you're working with them inside of your group. Okay? All right, the next thing, this is where I start getting them to sell me. Like, why do you think you'd be a good fit for the group? Okay, so I start getting them just to go as crazy as much as possible to sell them. If they write back in the application, like, I'd be awesome, I'd just ignore them. This question actually came from the very first Mastermind group I ever joined. It was almost 15 years ago. And it was Dan Kennedy and Bill Glazer's Mastermind group. I was like, no, I need to be in, I kept calling him, texting him, and faxing him, and like sending him gifts and pizzas in the mail, like anything I could to get their attention. One day I got an email back like, fine, we'll let you in the group, here's your application, send the money in, and we'll see in two weeks at the next meeting. I was like, sweet, this is awesome. I looked at the page and it was $25,000. I was like, what? Like literally to that point in my business, that only made about $25,000. I was like, 100% of all revenue had ever generated from any of my businesses and that's when I wanted to join this group. And guess what I didn't do? I didn't wait for them to sell me on it. So that's what this question is all about, is like selling, like getting them to sell you on why you should allow them through the doors. But if it's about $100, it's like, okay, that's kind of the sweet spot. We get $100 per application.
